<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Shinbutsu bunri]][[de:Shinbutsu-Bunri]][[en:Shinbutsu bunri]][[es:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fa:شینبوتسو بونری]][[fi:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fr:Shinbutsu bunri]][[id:Shinbutsu bunri]][[it:Shinbutsu bunri]][[ja:神仏分離]][[ru:Симбуцу бунри]][[tr:Şinbutsu-bunri]][[zh:神佛分離]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1970535]]
Wikidata name: shinbutsu bunri
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|The separation of Shinto from Buddhism}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

==See also==
* [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]
* [[Glossary of Shinto]]

==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}

{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Shinbutsu bunri]][[de:Shinbutsu-Bunri]][[en:Shinbutsu bunri]][[es:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fa:شینبوتسو بونری]][[fi:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fr:Shinbutsu bunri]][[id:Shinbutsu bunri]][[it:Shinbutsu bunri]][[ja:神仏分離]][[ru:Симбуцу бунри]][[tr:Şinbutsu-bunri]][[zh:神佛分離]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1970535]]
Wikidata name: shinbutsu bunri
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|The separation of Shinto from Buddhism}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

{{Short description|The separation of Shinto from Buddhism}}

{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Shinbutsu bunri]][[de:Shinbutsu-Bunri]][[en:Shinbutsu bunri]][[es:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fa:شینبوتسو بونری]][[fi:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fr:Shinbutsu bunri]][[id:Shinbutsu bunri]][[it:Shinbutsu bunri]][[ja:神仏分離]][[ru:Симбуцу бунри]][[tr:Şinbutsu-bunri]][[zh:神佛分離]]

Wikidata name: shinbutsu bunri
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|The separation of Shinto from Buddhism}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

Wikidata name: shinbutsu bunri
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|The separation of Shinto from Buddhism}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Shinbutsu bunri]][[de:Shinbutsu-Bunri]][[en:Shinbutsu bunri]][[es:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fa:شینبوتسو بونری]][[fi:Shinbutsu bunri]][[fr:Shinbutsu bunri]][[id:Shinbutsu bunri]][[it:Shinbutsu bunri]][[ja:神仏分離]][[ru:Симбуцу бунри]][[tr:Şinbutsu-bunri]][[zh:神佛分離]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1970535]]
Wikidata name: shinbutsu bunri
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|The separation of Shinto from Buddhism}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{more footnotes|date=June 2014}}
[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.

[[File:Turu daitoz.jpg|thumb|275 px|A Buddhist [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] (a [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]]-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] (薬師堂) at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] shrine in Kamakura before the ''shinbutsu bunri'']]
The Japanese term {{Nihongo|'''''shinbutsu bunri'''''|神仏分離}} indicates the separation of [[Shinto]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]] which separated Shinto ''[[kami]]'' from [[:en:Buddhism|buddhas]], and also [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] from [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, which were [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|originally amalgamated]]. It is a [[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]] phrase.

== Background before 1868 ==
Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in 1868, Shinto and Buddhism were intimately connected in what was called ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' (神仏習合), to the point that the same buildings were often used as both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, and Shinto gods were interpreted as manifestations of Buddhas. However, the tendency to oppose Buddhism as a foreign import and to uphold Shinto as the native religion can be seen already during the early modern era, partly as a nationalistic reaction.<ref name="EoSSB">{{citation | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto | title = Shinbutsu Bunri}}.</ref> In a broad sense, the term ''shinbutsu bunri'' indicates the effects of the anti-Buddhist movement that, from the middle of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] onwards, accompanied the spread of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], the growth of studies of ancient Japanese literature and culture (''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]])'', and the rise of Shinto-based nationalism,{{sfn|Stone |1993}} All these movements had reasons to oppose Buddhism.

==Policy of the Meiji government==
In a narrower sense, ''shinbutsu bunri'' refers to the policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism pursued by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]] with the {{nihongo |''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}} of 1868.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Josephson|first=Jason Ānanda|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/812924947|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-41235-1|location=Chicago|pages=150–152|oclc=812924947}}</ref> This order triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement that caused the forcible closure of thousands of temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return of many monks to lay life or their transformation into Shinto priests, and the destruction of numerous books, statues and other Buddhist artefacts.{{sfn|Stone |1993}}<ref name= "EoSHK">{{citation | title = Haibutsukishaku | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref> Even bronze bells were melted down to make cannons.{{sfn|Stone |1993}} However, the process of separation stalled by 1873, the government's intervention in support of the order was relaxed, and even today the separation is still only partially complete: many major Buddhist temples retain small shrines dedicated to tutelary Shinto ''kami'', and some Buddhist figures, such as the Bodhisattva [[Kannon]], are revered in Shinto shrines.<ref name="Scheid">{{citation | last = Scheid | first = Grundbegriffe | title = Shinto}}.</ref> The policy failed in its short-term aims and was ultimately abandoned, but it was successful in the long term in creating a new religious status quo in which Shinto and Buddhism are perceived as different and independent.

== Details of the policy ==
The new government that seized power in 1868 saw ''shinbutsu bunri'' as a way to reduce the immense wealth and power of the Buddhist sects. At the same time, it was supposed to give Shinto, and especially its cult of the Emperor, time to grow into an effective vehicle for nationalism.<ref name=":0" />

A first order issued by the Jinguji Muka in April 1868 ordered the defrocking of ''shasō'' and ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō]]'' (shrine monks performing Buddhist rites at Shinto shrines).<ref name= "EoSSB" />{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}}

A few days later, the Daijōkan banned the application of Buddhist terminology such as ''[[gongen]]'' to Japanese ''[[kami]]'' and the veneration of Buddhist statues in shrines.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

Next came a ban on applying the Buddhist term ''Daibosatsu'' (Great [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]]) to the syncretic ''kami'' [[Hachiman]] at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] and [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] shrines.<ref name = "EoSSB" />

In the final stage, all the defrocked ''bettō'' and ''shasō'' were told to become "shrine priests" (''[[kannushi]]'') and return to their shrines.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> Also, monks of the [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]] sect were told not to refer to some deities as ''kami''.<ref name="EoSSB"/>

== Consequences of the policy ==
The campaign ultimately failed to destroy the influence of Buddhism on the Japanese people, who still needed funerals, graves and ancestral rites,{{sfn|Hardacre |1986 | p= 42}} all services traditionally provided by Buddhism. The state's first attempt to influence religious life therefore resulted in failure.{{sfn |Hardacre |1986 | p = 43}} In 1873, the government admitted that the effort to elevate Shinto above Buddhism had failed.{{sfn|Burkman |1974|p=175}} However, the government did cause the diffusion of the idea that Shinto was the true religion of the Japanese, finally revealed after remaining for a long time hidden behind Buddhism.<ref name = "Scheid" />

In recent years, many historians have come to believe that the syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and Buddhas (''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]'') was just as authentically Japanese.<ref name ="Scheid"/> The government was successful in creating the impression that Shinto and Buddhism in Japan are completely independent religions. Most Japanese today are unaware that some of their customary religious practices cannot be understood outside the context of the syncretism of [[kami]] and Buddhas.{{sfn |Grapard | 1984 | p = 246}} In discussing some [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Japanese Buddhist temples]] dedicated to the cult of ''kami'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], Shinto scholar Karen Smyers comments:

{{blockquote |Recent scholarship has shown the term [Shinto]to be highly problematic – its current content is largely a political construction of the Meiji period. [...] The surprise of many of my informants regarding the existence of Buddhist Inari temples shows the success of the government's attempt to create separate conceptual categories regarding sites and certain identities, although practice remains multiple and nonexclusive.{{sfn |Smyers |1999|p = 219}}}}

==''Haibutsu kishaku''==
{{main|Haibutsu kishaku}}
{{see also|Koshintō|Shinbutsu kakuri}}
Although the government did not explicitly order the closing of temples, the destruction of Buddhist property or the defrocking of Buddhist priests and nuns, they were often interpreted as implying it, and the ''haibutsu kishaku'' movement soon spread across the country.<ref name= "EoSSB" /> The ''shinbutsu bunri'' policy was itself the direct cause of serious damage to important cultural properties. Because mixing the two religions was now forbidden, shrines and temples had to give away some of their treasures.<ref name="KOT">{{nihongo |''Kamakura Official Textbook for Culture and Tourism''|「鎌倉観光文化検定公式テキストブック」}}. {{in lang|ja}}</ref> For example, the giant {{nihongo|[[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]|仁王}}, wooden statues of guardian beings, at the entrance of the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]], a shrine in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], were objects of Buddhist worship and therefore illegal where they were, so they were sold to [[:en:Jufuku-ji|Jufuku-ji]], where they still stand today.{{sfn |Mutsu|1995| p = 172}} The shrine also had to destroy Buddhism-related buildings, for example its ''[[tahōtō]]'' tower, its {{nihongo|''midō''|御堂}}, and its {{nihongo|''[[shichidō garan]]''|七堂伽藍}}.<ref name = "KOT" /> Many Buddhist temples were simply closed, for example Zenkō-ji, to which the now-independent [[Meigetsu-in]] used to belong.

Another consequence of the policy was the creation of so-called "invented traditions".<ref name=":0" />{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} To avoid the destruction of material illegal under the new rules, Shinto and Buddhist priests invented traditions, genealogies and other information that justified its presence.{{sfn |Smyers|1999| p= 26–27}} Later, awareness of their origin was often lost, causing considerable confusion among historians.
